speaker: interviewer
text: --in England. And this is tape 7.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of them laid down with us and wanted to fumble with us. But then he realized that it was no good, that we weren't really women worthwhile looking at, and got up. And I had picked up a few words of Russian. And so I could-- and anyway they could see. But I could explain a little bit who we were. And they went off, these five. And they came back with a, a-- the-- another man who spoke-- another Russian who spoke German. And he, then, he obviously was their commandant. And, and he said-- sent them off to get stretchers and take us to a farm. What these people were-- these people were a patrol from the-- who, who were-- some of them were working as slave-- as, as forced workers, forced laborers at some farms. And the farmers were very bad to them. And the farmers were hiding now-- started-- were disappearing now. And so they were looking in all the barns and everywhere to catch these farmers because they wanted to string them up. And, instead of finding the farmers, they found us. So they took us to this farm. And they took us on to the first floor. There was some straw there. And-- not, not a room really, but it was a loft sort of thing. And they said-- this, this commandant said that they have to go away next morning at 6 o'clock. They have to be on the other side of the Elbe, back with the Russian troops. They got only permission to try and chase out some of the farmers-- they came over. But they will take us with them and will repatriate us. Now, I had an amnesia. I had a partial amnesia. And, I mean, I didn't remember lots of things. I didn't remember names. I didn't remember-- lots of things I didn't remember. But there was one thing I knew, that I was not going to go back to Hungary. Under no circumstances did I want to go back to Hungary. And so I dug myself in to straw-- during the night, I, I went in first. And I dug myself in the straw. I knew that if they don't find me soon, they have to leave. And if I can hold out until then, then I'm safe. What's going to happen to me afterwards, I didn't know. But I knew that I didn't want to go with the Russians back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did go-- I did go to-- I, I, I stayed there. The two others left. They wanted to go back anyway. And, and, and I was there-- left there alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it was clear that they had gone-- I, I heard no more voices-- I managed to crawl to a window. And there was a courtyard down there-- and with a well. And there were 10 men at the well. And they were speaking French. And I somehow managed to make myself heard. And then they came up. And I spoke to them and explained to them what has happened to me. And they were ex-prisoners of war who were now-- now everything was chaotic. It was roaming around. By the way, the day when I collapsed was my 21st birthday. It was the 23rd of April, 1945. And that was the day when I collapsed. And, and I-- so I was again right in my prediction. I wasn't sure that I would be there. But I, I was there. And I couldn't walk. I couldn't get up. And these men said that they are going to the American liberation center, which is 29 kilometers from there. And, well, I couldn't walk. There was no way I could get onto my feet. And so I said, I'm sorry, I would love to come with them, but I can't. And then, as they were leaving, one of them said he's not leaving. He's going to look after me. And he stayed. And he really looked after me. And he became the first man in my life. And I was-- I was very much in love because he was a human being who-- five years he was as prisoner of war-- or four-- and now, instead of rushing home, is looking after me. And he is seeing that I get onto to my feet. And, well, it seemed that we were both very much in love. Because I was telling him what happened. And my spirit was somehow still, still there. And anyway, I-- he said, would I marry him. Now I said already before that French-- everything that's French, for me, was fantastic-- French films, French-- even this-- And there was another thing, that in camp I found that actually the simple people, the uneducated people, they were more decent than the sophisticated-- more sophisticated people. And this was a completely unsophisticated, uneducated man. And he was French and spoke beautiful French. And I-- and he was decent to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I really fell in love. And I was very happy. I am going to go to France. And I am going to get married. And everything-- life is going to be wonderful from now on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so a, a few days later, on the 29th of April, I managed to get enough on my feet that, with his help and a stick, I could walk a little bit. And we started to walk. And then a tractor came with two open lorries. And on these two open lorries, something miraculous happened. Because there was a French flag. And there were 38 French people-- 10 French women who came from concentration camp and 28 men who were prisoners of war-- who were on this lorry pulled by the tractor. And because my companion was French, they took us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were now a group of 40 people. And a group of 40 people with transport, we arrived in Wurzen, which was the, the-- it was-- should I spell it perhaps? W-U-R-Z-E-N, which was a town where there was a liberation-- American liberation center.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were officially liberated. I don't know all dates taken-- everything. And they said because we are a group of 40, and because we have our own transport, they won't retain us at Wurzen or won't send us in DP camps. But we can move on. And they gave us vouchers to requisition food. And they gave us vouchers to requisition lodging. And so we moved on. And a few days later, we arrived in Metz, which was the official French reception center for people. And we were the first 11 women, 11 French women-- I wasn't French, but 11 women-- who came from a concentration camp. Now because I wasn't French and because I wasn't going to go to Paris, but I, I was with this man, who was from Toulon, I-- they, they didn't send me away. But the other 10 women, they sent them by airplane to Paris. And I wasn't-- I, I was taken-- I mean, I went with the troops, with the prisoners of war and with my fiance slowly. It took us about three-and-a-half weeks to get through France. Wherever we went, we got lots of money, lots of clothing, especially I got lots of things because I was from camps. The others were prisoners of war with them. And I, I, I was a woman with all men, really. And, and they were incredibly generous. And eventually, we arrived in Toulon. We spent-- to show that how, how my, my memory was bad, we spent three days in Paris. And I didn't remember that I had an uncle there, whom I adored, by the way. And I had no idea that I had an uncle. Later on, when I returned to Paris I found out that I was one street away from him. [LAUGHS] And anyway, I didn't know. And by the time I arrived in-- well, in Toulon, we were received, first, by the Red Cross. And they interviewed us and examined us. And then they-- then they, they told this man that he can go home to his mother, I mean, to his home, and he-- and take me, of course. Because he guaranteed-- that everywhere he wrote, he signed that I am his fiancee and that I am coming with him. I had no number. I, I grew bigger and bigger. I was 39 kilogram when I collapsed. And now I was growing bigger and bigger until I went up to 98 kilogram.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were eating or because of other-- an illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, no, it turned out that it was a so-called something. In French, they call it [FRENCH]. I have no idea what-- it's some anemia. And also, I had heart trouble. But I didn't know it. And I, I-- so as that-- and I always kept my red cheeks so that I looked very healthy. I had no, no papers. I had no tattoo. There was nothing-- I spoke fluent German. There was nothing to show that I wasn't German. Anyway, and so-- but he guaranteed for me everywhere. He signed, and he guaranteed for me. And then he said that he thinks because he-- they didn't-- his mother didn't know that he was coming home, he thinks it's better if he goes first alone and then he comes back to fetch me. But he wants to come first alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I stay with the Red Cross for an hour or so? Well, an hour passed. And two hours passed. And three hours passed. And he didn't come. Eventually, they sent the police for him and he had to take me home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Home was in Toulon, a building. We were on the third floor. And on the third floor, there, there was a tiny kitchen, where there was a, a, a, a curtain partition. And behind this curtain partition, there were two buckets for human needs. And these were emptied into the courtyard through the window. There was a-- the man had a 16-year-old very beautiful, blond sister. And there was Mama, who was not very beautiful. And, and, and he said, well, he has to do quite a lot of things. So I stayed with Mama. And Mama was anything but kind to me or was very pleased with me. She didn't think it was a very good idea that he brought me home. And he disappeared. For the next four days, I didn't see him. And in the evening, Mama and sister took me down to the port to show me the port, where there were lights and there were Negroes and there were sailors. And it was all, like, in a film. I mean it was interesting. It was an adventure.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my fiance wasn't there. And I was rather upset. And when other men-- and there was dancing-- and when other men wanted to dance with me, I always said no. And I didn't drink because I said, I don't want to drink without my fiance. I want to drink with my fiance. Where is he? And that went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during the day, Mama washed, was a washer woman and also shared an allotment. And we went to the allotment and picked, picked vegetables and made ravioli-- because they were half-Italian-- and made ravioli at home. And I learned to make ravioli. And I helped her. I was willing to help with absolutely everything. But the only thing was I was constantly saying, I want, want to see my fiance. And anyway, I-- in that bar, Mama and sister from time to time disappeared. And I didn't know. I was-- I was incredibly naive. I was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and it took me, I think, three days until it dawned on me, why do these men want to dance with me? And why-- how is it that these women constantly disappear? They dance with a man. And then they don't come back, but suddenly disappear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and Mama got angrier and angrier that I refuse to dance with any of the men. And, well, it wasn't a very nice atmosphere. And on the fourth or fifth day, I said to Mama, look, get me Charles because I want to speak to him. I want to go away. I don't want to stay here. There's no point. I'm not doing what you want me to do. Because by that time, I realized that they want me-- wanted me to behave like a prostitute as well. And they-- so they-- first, they, they said I can't see him. And I made, I don't know, a tantrum or something. And anyway, they produced him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I told him, look, since I know French and I'm a nursery nurse and a nursery-school teacher, why don't you take me back to these nice Red Cross people? And I'm sure they will be able to get a job for me. Again, naive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he said, fine. This is a good idea. And I said, then I will leave. And, and that's the end of it. He said, well, put on your Buchenwald jacket-- the jacket which I got from this man-- and your shirt and don't, don't put on your clothes which are nice clothes which you have got now. And let us go so that they recognize you. And that I did. And then we went to the station. And at the station he said, wait a second. I just see whether they are here. And a minute or so later came back with two policemen, who handcuffed me and put me into a cell. And that gave me such a shock that my amnesia disappeared. I suddenly started remembering names. I suddenly remembered the name of the Frenchman who worked down in the offloading bay. I remembered my uncle's name. I remembered my, my-- I had-- well, my aunt's name, who from Vienna, went to Mexico and then to New York. My-- another aunt's name who was in Switzerland, in Lausanne-- I didn't know the addresses, but the names at least. And so-- and, and I was-- while I was sitting there, I found a piece of glass. And I started cutting. I have got still a little somewhere here-- yes, here it is. I started cutting it. I wasn't very successful. It wasn't at-- quite at the right place. And anyway, it was a very thick piece of glass. And eventually, I was bleeding a little bit, but not really by the time I was taken in for interrogation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it turned out-- and I said, what's happening? And they said, well, you are an SS woman. Obviously, you are a German SS woman. And you are-- this man said that he made a mistake. And, and I said, well, I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by that time, because this gave me such a shock, I, I remembered these names. And I gave the names of these people. And I said, you can check up on these people that I am telling you the truth. I'm Jewish. I was in concentration camp. I am not tattooed. But my number was such and such. And you can verify all this. Anyway, they verified it. But in the meantime, from Toulon they took me to Bandol for three weeks, and from Bandol to Prison Saint Pierre in Marseilles. And I was together with French Miliciens, who were the French Nazis, and, and German Nazis, and Italian fascists. And in Marseilles, I was four months in prison. And the way I got out of prison is another interesting story, which I don't know-- which I would like to tell. I-- one-- well, there was one policewoman who was very kind to me. I did not speak to most of the prisoners. I refused to speak. Also, they nearly all had money. And they went, and they could bribe the guards. And they went out to the beach. And they bought food, nice food. And they were quite well off. In the prison, we had rats, which was not very pleasant. And this policewoman occasionally spoke to me. And she was very nice. And she-- I had a feeling that she did believe my story, up to a certain extent. And then, one day, it-- I had toothache. And, and she-- they told me I'm going to be taken to the hospital, to the dentist. And she took me. And we had a long wait. And while we were waiting, in walked a man on crutches, and another man with him. And I looked at this man on crutches. I looked. And I looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I said to this policewoman, would you allow me to ask a question from this man? And she said, you don't know him. What do you want to ask him? And I said, well, if you let me, then you will hear. And so I, I said, were you, on the 7th of April, in a camp-- in a camp called Thekla in Leipzig? And he looks at me. And he says, Gertrude, [FRENCH], what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I told him my story, what happened to me. And he said, well, don't worry. And this was the man who gave me, in Thekla camp, the jacket and the soap, and who came four times when we stopped to look for me if I'm still there. And then he disappeared. And this was the same man. And he actually went back. He was in a convalescent home near Marseilles, in Camoins les Bains. And he, he went up there. And in the afternoon, about 20 of them came down and brought me a big parcel with food that they said, you won't have time to eat it all because we get you out. Don't worry. We get you out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what happened? Toulon is in the county of Var. And, and Marseilles is the county of Bouches-du-Rhone. And the prefect of the county of Var had two sons who were killed in the Resistance. And he absolutely refused to believe my story and refused to hand over my, my papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all knew already. They had-- in Marseilles, they had the answers from my aunts. From everybody they had the answers. And they knew every word I said was true. But they couldn't let me out until they didn't get my file from Toulon. And this man was sitting on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next day, the man, the-- this, this prisoner, this cripple, took-- came down to the camp with 50 men. They put on all Buchenwald clothes, jackets. And 50 of them went to Toulon and got my file and brought it back. And in the afternoon, they took me out. They freed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The prefect lost his job on my account. And, and they-- and they took me up to their convalescent home and wanted me to, to be there. In the meantime, I was already in correspondence with my uncle in Paris, who very much wanted me to come to Paris. And I was dying to come to Paris. And, and they-- the, the French-- the prison authorities apologized [LAUGHS] for having me locked up all this time. And I, I was eating prison food. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Well, during this time, I already corresponded with, with all the people whose name I gave and whose names I found out. And I, I corresponded with my uncle in Paris. And I was dying to go up to Paris. And, and he, he was writing, really, most welcoming letters to me. And I-- and-- but these people said, well, why don't you stay for-- at least, you should-- you need convalescing now. And you should be staying up here. And so I agreed to stay for a couple of weeks. And I, I stayed up there. And then I went up to Paris. And in Paris, I found that my uncle was a very poor man. Now, my uncle was my uncle because he married a sister of my mother, who was run over by a, a, a, a coach, by a bus, in 1935. So that by that time, really, he wasn't my uncle anymore. He was-- he was not Jewish. He was married-- he had another wife. But the flat was absolutely full with her pictures, with my aunt's pictures. And during the war, he was hiding and helping some of the family, some of my cousins who came through Paris. And he was absolutely wonderful. He behaved wonderfully. And now, he had-- he was 62. And he had a wife who was 35. And, and they were wonderful to me. They were extremely poor. And they were also very bohemian. I mean, I can't go in to tell about my uncle, which is a lovely story. But it was-- they were so nice to me. And they really didn't have any money. And they were so nice to me that I felt I had to do something. Now, the, the flat was pretty, pretty dirty. And it was full with very interesting mementos. He traveled around a lot in his life and did all sorts of things in his life. And he was a highly intelligent man. But he, he was-- the flat was not very clean. And I wanted to show my, my thankfulness. And so I started cleaning the flat, which was the worst I could do. Because obviously his wife [LAUGHS] didn't think it was-- it was for me to show her up, which I certainly didn't want to because I, I, I loved her. She was lovely to me. But things became very tense. But I was anyway already looking for a job because I wanted to. I didn't want to be on their back. I was only for short while with them. They said that I can stay forever. But I-- there-- it was evident that I'm not going to stay. And then, of course, things got tense. And I got the job with [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH], French [NON-ENGLISH], had a, a children's home out in Montmorency. Now, this children's home was run by two non-Jewish women who actually were hiding-- hid during the war 40 Jewish children whose parents were deported and whose parents didn't come back. And they were-- and one of these children was a very interesting case. The children were between 5-- I think between 5 and 15. And there was only one 15-year-old. And the 15-year-old was the following-- his mother was a French Jewess. His father was a German SS. And the father-- and there were rumors that the father had his mother deported to get rid of her. But he put the boy, because he was half-Jewish, into this-- and to make sure-- and he protected this home. And that's how the other children were also protected. And therefore-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and these two women really looked after these children wonderfully during the whole war and, and were very, very good to them. And, and then-- and suddenly, [NON-ENGLISH] put me, a 21-year-old girl-- 21-and-a-half-year-old girl in charge of them, which was as insensitive as it can just be. I mean, I was very proud. And I was-- I thought it was wonderful. And I didn't quite understand first what has happened to me. But, of course, these two women hated me. I-- it's quite obvious. For, for four years, they were doing their best to look after these children. Suddenly, they send them out someone and put someone over them, a 21-year-old girl, which is absolutely terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't-- we didn't have-- also, we had terrible shortages. We had hardly anything to heat with. We had-- we had hardly any potatoes, hardly any food, and no soap. No medical men.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- at one point, I had 12 children ill. And I, I slept with them because I felt that they needed care. I mean, somebody needed to be with them during the night because some had really high temperature and everything. I was-- when I took on the job, they told me that once a week, I will have a day free. And once a week, I will have an afternoon free. And, and after six weeks I didn't have one minute free. And I was on the verge of collapse. And I phoned and phoned and phoned. And eventually, they send out a 16-year-old girl to help me, who needed looking after even more, who was-- not only that she was cheeky and didn't want to work, but she was really more a hindrance than anything else. And then I had this terribly difficult time with this child, with this 15-year-old boy.
